content.plain_english: This week your child is using maths words to describe where things are and how they move — left, right, forwards, backwards, clockwise, anti-clockwise. They are also learning that a quarter turn is a right angle.
content.in_school: Children move themselves and small toys around grids and routes drawn on the floor or paper. They follow and give instructions like 'turn clockwise a quarter turn, then walk three steps forwards'. They draw arrows for direction and use right-angle markers to spot quarter turns in shapes.
content.home_activity: On the walk home one afternoon, take turns giving each other directions: 'turn left at the post box, walk forwards five paces, stop'. At home, give your child a soft toy and ask them to make it do a quarter turn clockwise, then a half turn anti-clockwise. Two minutes is enough.
dinner_table_questions: If you face the front door and turn a quarter turn to your right, what are you looking at now?
How many quarter turns make a full turn?
What is the difference between clockwise and anti-clockwise?

key_vocabulary.definition: Where something is. We describe it using words like above, below, next to, in front of, behind.
Which way something is facing or moving. Words like left, right, forwards, backwards.
Moving in the same direction as the hands of a clock.
Moving in the opposite direction to the hands of a clock.
A square corner. A quarter turn is a right angle. Two right angles make a half turn.

example_questions: How many right angles are there in a square?
If you start facing north and turn a half turn clockwise, which direction are you facing now?
Describe how to get from your bedroom to the kitchen using only the words left, right and forwards.
content.times_table_tip: Four right angles make a full turn. Counting in 4s — 4, 8, 12, 16 — helps your child move smoothly between quarter, half, three-quarter and full turns.
